attributes.stem:
A 5-month-old boy is brought into the General Practice by his first time mother. His mother reports he has frequent non-forceful 'spit ups' during the day, that have slowly increased in frequency over the past month. They occur intermittently throughout the day, most commonly after settling post feeds. The vomitus is the colour of his feeds and show no signs of blood.  His mother states that he latches on well and has good appetite. He is breastfed with occasional top ups with formula since 1 month old.

He was born at term with no complications, measuring above the 60th percentile for both height and weight and is up to date with his immunisations. He is now at the 50th percentile for weight and 54th for height.

He has reached appropriate developmental milestones for his age, physical examination is unremarkable. The mother is wanting to begin trying solid foods with her son next month and is concerned he will not be able to tolerate this.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following is the most appropriate management plan?
attributes.explanation:
This infant presents with non-forceful reflux throughout the day (not explicitly associated with feeds) and without any developmental consequences i.e. no weight loss or growth restriction. This fits the picture of gastro-oesophageal reflux (GOR) which is a normal physiological occurrence in infancy characterised by effortless reflux of stomach contents into the oesophagus and sometimes mouth. GER is differentiated from gastro-oesophageal reflux disease (GORD) by the absence of consequences of reflux such as weight loss and oesophagitis.

Switching to a different formula would be recommended in a patient presenting with Cows Milk Protein Allergy (CMPA). These patients typically present within days to weeks on introduction of cows milk-based formula with symptoms like GER and eosinophilic gastroenteritis, abdominal pain and subsequently failure to thrive and weight loss. They may also present with protein-induced proctocolitis with bloody-streaked loose-stools. The clinical picture does not fit that of CMPA.

Reassurance is indicated as this is a normal physiological process that often peaks around four months and often resolves by 12 months. Education about practices to reduce reflux i.e. sitting up after meals, would also be indicated.

With regards to the percentile change, it has been estimated as many as 30% of normal children crossed one major percentile line and 23% crossed two in the first two years of life. In other words, the mother can be reassured that this change in percentile is not an issue.

A limited trial of a proton pump inhibitor (PPI) (eg esomeprazole) would only be indicated in a case of GORD if the patient was demonstrating poor weight gain and esophagitis, which is not present in this scenario. Additionally a trial of lifestyle modifications should be attempted first before PPI treatment in this setting.

Immunological testing can be used to help diagnose IgE mediated CMPA, however as discussed this scenario does not depict CMPA.

Pyloromyotomy is the definitive treatment for infantile hypertrophic pyloric stenosis. This condition typically presents with forceful, non-bilious vomiting straight after a meal. In a majority of patients an 'olive-like' mass can be palpated in the right upper quadrant. This patient has intermittent reflux throughout the day, not just after meals, and is non-forceful making GOR more likely. Additionally, despite this patients symptoms occurring for a month his height and weight are appropriate, which may not be the case in pyloric stenosis.
learningPoints: Gastro-oesophageal reflux is a normal physiological process in infancy that usually self-resolves within 12 months of life.
Gastro-oesophageal reflux in infancy requires no intervention if there is no failure to thrive or complications such as oesophagitis present.
references: Portal, MR. Gastroesophageal reflux disease in infants [Internet]. RCH; [Updated 2019; cited 2021 Sep 18]. Available from: <https://www.rch.org.au/clinicalguide/guideline_index/Gastrooesophageal_reflux_disease_in_infants/>

Winter, HS. Gastroesophageal reflux in infants [Internet]. Uptodate; 2021 [Updated 2021; cited 2021 Sep 18]. Available from: <https://www-uptodate-com.ezproxy.flinders.edu.au/contents/gastroesophageal-reflux-in-infants?search=reflux%20in%20infants&source=search_result&selectedTitle=1~150&usage_type=default&display_rank=1#H1>

Jarvinen-Seppo, KM. Milk allergy: Clinical features and diagnosis [Internet]. Uptodate; 2021 [Updated 2021; cited 2021 Sep 18]. Available from: <https://www-uptodate-com.ezproxy.flinders.edu.au/contents/milk-allergy-clinical-features-and-diagnosis?search=cows%20milk%20protein%20intolerance%20children&source=search_result&selectedTitle=2~46&usage_type=default&display_rank=2#H6>.
Allen K, Ho SS. Gastro-oesophageal reflux in children: What's the worry?. Australian family physician. 2012 May;41(5):268-72.

Olivé, AP and Endom, EE. Infantile hypertrophic pyloric stenosis [Internet]. Uptodate; 2020 [Updated 2020; cited 2021 Sep 18]. Available from: <https://www-uptodate-com.ezproxy.flinders.edu.au/contents/infantile-hypertrophic-pyloric-stenosis?search=pylori%20stenosis&source=search_result&selectedTitle=1~74&usage_type=default&display_rank=1#H12171761>.
